channel.title: Nature Materials
channel.description: Nature Materials is a multidisciplinary journal aimed at bringing together cutting-edge research across the entire spectrum of materials science and technology. Nature Materials covers all applied and fundamental aspects of the synthesis/processing, structure/composition, properties and performance of materials. Nature Materials provides a forum for the development of a common identity among materials scientists while encouraging researchers to cross established subdisciplinary lines. To achieve this, Nature Materials takes an interdisciplinary, integrated and balanced approach to all areas of materials research while fostering the exchange of ideas between scientists involved in different communities.
channel.dc:rights: © 2025 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2025 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Decoupling shear from grain boundary migration
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 17 December 2025; doi:10.1038/s41563-025-02457-7

Decoupling shear from grain boundary migration
item.dc:title: Decoupling shear from grain boundary migration
item.dc:creator: Xin Li

item.title: Speeding up antiferromagnetic switching
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 17 December 2025; doi:10.1038/s41563-025-02454-w

Room-temperature non-volatile antiferromagnetic switching in 140 picoseconds is demonstrated in a nanometre-thick metallic material.
item.dc:title: Speeding up antiferromagnetic switching
item.dc:creator: Davide Bossini

item.title: Probing frozen solid electrolyte interphases
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 17 December 2025; doi:10.1038/s41563-025-02443-z

Probing frozen solid electrolyte interphases
item.dc:title: Probing frozen solid electrolyte interphases
item.dc:creator: Saunak Das

item.title: Healing crystals without heat
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 17 December 2025; doi:10.1038/s41563-025-02455-9

An organic crystal repairs itself, even in liquid nitrogen, by using a dipole-driven mechanism rather than relying on thermal diffusion.
item.dc:title: Healing crystals without heat
item.dc:creator: Kalyan J. Kalita
Catharine Esterhuysen
Leonard J. Barbour

item.title: Materials reach new levels of frustration
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 16 December 2025; doi:10.1038/s41563-025-02442-0

The small-bandgap semiconductors LnCd3P3 (Ln = La, Ce, Pr and Nd) are promising materials to study emergent phenomena from geometric frustration across bond, spin and charge degrees of freedom.
item.dc:title: Materials reach new levels of frustration
item.dc:creator: Aravind Devarakonda

item.title: Reconfiguring perovskite interlayers
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 16 December 2025; doi:10.1038/s41563-025-02438-w

A thin coherent interlayer on polycrystalline perovskites prompts the formation of uniform, orderly fullerene films with high coverage while suppressing dimerization enabling efficient and stable perovskite photovoltaics.
item.dc:title: Reconfiguring perovskite interlayers
item.dc:creator: Yu Zou
Yixin Zhao

item.title: Geometry-driven polarity in antiferromagnetic metals
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 16 December 2025; doi:10.1038/s41563-025-02449-7

A polar antiferromagnetic metal is realized in Sr3Co2O7 through geometry-driven inversion-symmetry breaking, and an anomalous Hall effect is observed.
item.dc:title: Geometry-driven polarity in antiferromagnetic metals
item.dc:creator: Qihan Zhang
Jingsheng Chen
